section.title: Do Not Fret Because of the Wicked
section.sectionRationale: A wisdom acrostic psalm, each strophe once paired to a letter of the Hebrew alphabet, wrestling with the perennial problem of the wicked's brief flourishing. It closes Book I, Psalms 1-41, returning to that book's opening contrast between the righteous and the wicked, but now from the vantage point of a long life, 'I once was young and now am old', verse 25.
section.thesis: David counsels against fretting or envying the wicked's fleeting prosperity, calling the righteous instead to trust, delight in, and wait for the LORD, who sees, judges justly, and will give the meek the land, a hope Jesus takes up directly when He blesses the meek with the inheritance of the earth.
section.frameNote: Eight movements following the acrostic's turning wheel: the call not to fret but trust, verses 1-6; be still and wait, verses 7-11; the wicked's schemes and their certain end, verses 12-17; the LORD's care for the blameless, verses 18-22; a lifetime's testimony that the righteous are not forsaken, verses 23-26; turning from evil to keep God's way, verses 27-31; the wicked lying in wait yet not prevailing, verses 32-36; and the closing contrast of the future of the wicked and the salvation of the righteous, verses 37-40.

units.label: Do not fret, but trust and delight in the Lord

verses.text: Do not fret over those who do evil; do not envy those who do wrong.
For they wither quickly like grass and wilt like tender plants.
Trust in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and cultivate faithfulness.
Delight yourself in the LORD, and He will give you the desires of your heart.
Commit your way to the LORD; trust in Him, and He will do it.
He will bring forth your righteousness like the dawn, your justice like the noonday sun.

units.label: Be still and wait patiently
units.note: the meek will inherit the land

verses.text: Be still before the LORD and wait patiently for Him; do not fret when men prosper in their ways, when they carry out wicked schemes.
Refrain from anger and abandon wrath; do not fret, it can only bring harm.
For the evildoers will be cut off, but those who {{qavah|hope}} in the LORD will inherit the land.
Yet a little while, and the wicked will be no more; though you look for them, they will not be found.
But the {{anavim|meek}} will inherit the land and delight in abundant prosperity.

units.label: The wicked scheme, but the Lord upholds the righteous
units.note: swords that pierce their own hearts

verses.text: The wicked scheme against the righteous and gnash their teeth at them,
but the Lord laughs, seeing that their day is coming.
The wicked have drawn the sword and bent the bow to bring down the poor and needy, to slay those whose ways are upright.
But their swords will pierce their own hearts, and their bows will be broken.
Better is the little of the righteous than the abundance of many who are wicked.
For the arms of the wicked will be broken, but the LORD upholds the righteous.

units.label: The Lord knows the days of the blameless

verses.text: The LORD knows the days of the blameless, and their inheritance will last forever.
In the time of evil they will not be ashamed, and in the days of famine they will be satisfied.
But the wicked and enemies of the LORD will perish like the glory of the fields. They will vanish; like smoke they will fade away.
The wicked borrow and do not repay, but the righteous are gracious and giving.
Surely those He blesses will inherit the land, but the cursed will be destroyed.

units.label: A lifetime's testimony
units.note: never have I seen the righteous abandoned

verses.text: The steps of a man are ordered by the LORD who takes delight in his journey.
Though he falls, he will not be overwhelmed, for the LORD is holding his hand.

I once was young and now am old, yet never have I seen the righteous abandoned 
or their children begging for bread.
They are ever generous and quick to lend, and their children are a blessing.

units.label: Turn from evil and do good
units.note: the law of God in the heart

verses.text: Turn away from evil and do good, so that you will abide forever.
For the LORD loves justice and will not forsake His {{chasidim|saints}}. They are preserved forever, but the offspring of the wicked will be cut off.
The righteous will inherit the land and dwell in it forever.
The mouth of the righteous man utters wisdom, and his tongue speaks justice.
The law of his God is in his heart; his steps do not falter.

units.label: The wicked lie in wait, yet do not prevail

verses.text: Though the wicked lie in wait for the righteous, and seek to slay them,
the LORD will not leave them in their power or let them be condemned under judgment.
{{qavah|Wait}} for the LORD and keep His way, and He will raise you up to inherit the land. When the wicked are cut off, you will see it.
I have seen a wicked, ruthless man flourishing like a well-rooted native tree,
yet he passed away and was no more; though I searched, he could not be found.

units.label: The future of the wicked and the salvation of the righteous

verses.text: Consider the blameless and observe the upright, for posterity awaits the man of peace.
But the transgressors will all be destroyed; the future of the wicked will be cut off.
The salvation of the righteous is from the LORD; He is their stronghold in time of trouble.

The LORD helps and delivers them; He rescues and saves them from the wicked, 
because they take refuge in Him.

qavah.gloss: To wait with expectant hope, from a root meaning to stretch or tense like a cord, picturing eager, taut waiting rather than passive resignation. The righteous hope in the LORD, verse 9, and are commanded to wait for Him, verse 34.

anavim.gloss: The meek, humble, or afflicted, those who have learned dependence on God rather than self-assertion. Jesus takes up this exact promise in Matthew 5:5, 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.'

chasidim.gloss: His saints or godly ones, from chesed, God's covenant loving devotion. Those bound to God in loyal love are themselves shaped by that love and will not be forsaken.

basic.context: Written from the vantage point of a long life, verse 25, this wisdom psalm answers a problem every generation faces: the wicked often seem to prosper while the righteous struggle. David's counsel is not to fret or envy but to trust, delight in, commit to, and wait for the LORD, confident that the wicked's flourishing is as brief as grass and that the meek will inherit the land.
basic.hermeneutics: This is wisdom literature, not a blanket promise of material prosperity to every righteous person in every circumstance. It calls for a settled trust that outlasts appearances, grounded in the LORD's justice being sure even when it is not yet visible.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm repeatedly uses qavah, to wait with taut, expectant hope, and commends the anavim, the meek or humble, who will inherit the land, the exact promise Jesus repeats in Matthew 5:5. It also calls the righteous chasidim, those shaped by God's chesed, His covenant loving devotion.
basic.typology: The land-inheritance promised to the meek and the righteous anticipates the wider, truer inheritance the New Testament describes, Abraham and his offspring inheriting the world, Romans 4:13, and finally the meek inheriting the earth in the kingdom Jesus proclaims, Matthew 5:5.
basic.christ: Jesus takes up this psalm's central promise word for word, 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth,' Matthew 5:5, reshaping Israel's land promise into the kingdom's full and final inheritance. He also lived the psalm's pattern perfectly, committing His way entirely to the Father, entrusting Himself to Him who judges justly even while suffering unjustly, 1 Peter 2:23.
basic.god: God is shown seeing every scheme of the wicked and laughing, not with cruelty but with the calm certainty of one who already knows how it ends, while at the same time ordering the steps of the righteous, delighting in their way, and holding their hand so that a fall never becomes a final ruin.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 5:5 takes up the promise that the meek will inherit the land, now the whole earth. Proverbs 3:5-6 echoes the call to trust the LORD rather than lean on one's own understanding. 1 Peter 2:23 shows Christ Himself entrusting His cause to God who judges justly, the pattern this psalm commends.
basic.oneStory: The wicked's flourishing is brief as grass, but the LORD upholds, sustains, and finally saves the righteous who trust, wait, and delight in Him, a hope brought to its fullest expression in Jesus, who blesses the meek with the inheritance of the earth and who Himself trusted the Father completely through suffering to glory.

context.literary: An acrostic wisdom psalm, each strophe originally keyed to a letter of the Hebrew alphabet, built from short, proverb-like couplets contrasting the wicked and the righteous, alternating command, 'do not fret,' 'trust,' 'wait,' with testimony, 'I have seen,' drawn from a long life of observation.
context.historical: Attributed to David, likely written in his later years, verse 25, addressing a community tempted to envy the apparent success of the wicked and to doubt whether righteousness truly pays.
context.canonical: Closes Book I of the Psalter, Psalms 1-41, returning to the contrast between the righteous and the wicked with which the whole Psalter opens in Psalm 1, now seasoned by a lifetime's experience of that contrast working itself out.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To counter envy and anxious fretting over the wicked's temporary prosperity by grounding the righteous in patient trust, wholehearted delight, and confident waiting on the LORD, who alone secures a lasting inheritance and salvation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm prescribes trust, delight, and patient waiting as the proper response to the wicked's apparent success; it does not promise that every righteous person will experience visible material prosperity in this life, but that the LORD's justice and care are certain, in this life and beyond it.

debates.question: Does this psalm promise material prosperity and long life to every righteous person, or a more durable, ultimately eternal inheritance?

views.label: The psalm speaks primarily of real, this-life outcomes David observed, land, provision, and children, as the ordinary pattern of God's care for the righteous within Israel's covenant life
views.case: The concrete language of dwelling in the land, verse 3, inheriting the land, verses 9, 11, 22, 29, 34, and never seeing the righteous begging bread, verse 25, reads most naturally as this-life covenant blessing, consistent with the Torah's blessings for obedience.

views.label: The psalm's promises point beyond any one person's earthly circumstances to God's ultimate justice and inheritance, since experience shows some righteous people do suffer want and some wicked people die prosperous
views.case: The psalm itself acknowledges that the wicked's judgment sometimes takes 'a little while,' verse 10, and Scripture elsewhere, Job, Psalm 73, wrestles honestly with righteous suffering and wicked prosperity, showing the ultimate resolution lies in God's final justice, which the New Testament locates in the coming kingdom.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that the wicked's advantage is temporary and the LORD's care for the righteous is certain; the debate concerns timing and form, not whether God's justice will finally prevail, and Jesus's own citation of this psalm's promise in Matthew 5:5 confirms it reaches its fullest truth in the kingdom of heaven.

study.originalLanguages: David commands qavah, expectant, taut hope in the LORD, twice, verses 9 and 34, and promises the land to the anavim, the meek or humble, the very word and promise Jesus repeats in Matthew 5:5. The righteous are called chasidim, shaped by God's chesed, His covenant loving devotion, which is why they will not finally be forsaken.

typology.type: The meek inheriting the land, verses 9, 11, 22, 29, 34
typology.fulfillment: Jesus's promise in Matthew 5:5, 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth,' widening Israel's land promise to the whole earth in the coming kingdom.

typology.type: Committing one's way entirely to the LORD, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own perfect trust, entrusting Himself to Him who judges justly even while suffering unjustly, 1 Peter 2:23.

study.christ: Jesus does not merely allude to this psalm, He quotes its central promise directly: 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth,' Matthew 5:5. In doing so He takes David's hard-won wisdom, forged over a long life of watching the wicked flourish and fade, and declares it the very shape of His kingdom. He Himself lived the psalm's ideal perfectly, committing His way to the Father, trusting Him to bring forth righteousness like the dawn, and entrusting Himself to God's just judgment through suffering to resurrection glory, 1 Peter 2:23.
study.god: God is revealed as the patient, all-seeing judge who laughs at the wicked's schemes because their end is already certain, and as the intimately attentive Father who orders the righteous person's steps, delights in their way, and holds their hand so that even a fall does not become a final ruin.

crossReferences.note: Jesus repeats this psalm's exact promise, that the meek will inherit the earth, at the heart of the Beatitudes.
Echoes this psalm's call to trust the LORD with the whole heart rather than leaning on one's own understanding.
Christ entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, the pattern of committing one's way to the LORD this psalm commends.

study.oneStory: The wicked's prosperity withers like grass, but the LORD upholds, sustains, and finally saves all who trust, delight in, and wait for Him, a hope Jesus brings to its fullest flowering when He blesses the meek with the inheritance of the whole earth and Himself walks the path of perfect trust from suffering to glory.

sermon.title: Battling the Unbelief of Envy
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Psalm 37:1-7, showing that envy of the wicked's prosperity is at root a failure to believe God's promises, and that trusting, delighting in, and committing our way to the LORD is the biblical answer to that unbelief.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say happens to those who do evil, despite their apparent success?

options.text: They wither quickly like grass and will soon be no more, though they seem to flourish now.
options.note: Verses 2, 10, and 20 all describe the wicked's prosperity as brief, like grass, smoke, or the glory of the fields.

options.text: They will prosper forever without any consequence.
options.note: The psalm consistently teaches the opposite, their prosperity is temporary.

options.text: They will be given the land as their permanent inheritance.
options.note: The land inheritance is promised to the meek and righteous, not the wicked, who will be cut off.

options.text: They will eventually repent and be restored.
options.note: The psalm speaks of the wicked being cut off and vanishing, not of their repentance and restoration.

quiz.q: According to verse 11, who will inherit the land and delight in abundant prosperity?

options.text: The meek, the anavim, those who are humble and dependent on God.
options.note: This is the exact promise Jesus repeats in Matthew 5:5, 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.'

options.text: The wealthy and powerful.
options.note: The psalm contrasts the meek with the wicked, not with the poor; wealth and power are not the criterion.

options.text: Only the priests and Levites.
options.note: The psalm addresses the righteous and meek in general, not a particular tribe or office.

options.text: Those who take vengeance on their enemies.
options.note: The psalm actually counsels refraining from anger and wrath, verse 8, not taking vengeance.

quiz.q: What lifelong testimony does the psalmist give in verse 25?

options.text: He has never seen the righteous abandoned or their children begging for bread.
options.note: This is the psalmist's personal, long-observed testimony to God's faithful care for the righteous.

options.text: He has seen many righteous people suffer complete ruin.
options.note: The testimony given is the opposite, a witness to the LORD's sustaining care over a lifetime.

options.text: He has never met a righteous person.
options.note: The testimony assumes the psalmist has observed many righteous people over a long life.

options.text: He expects to see the righteous suffer in his old age.
options.note: The testimony is retrospective confidence based on a lifetime of observation, not a future expectation of suffering.

quiz.q: How does Jesus directly connect Himself to this psalm's central promise?

options.text: He repeats its exact promise in Matthew 5:5, 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.'
options.note: Jesus takes the psalm's promise to the meek and widens it into a beatitude of His kingdom.

options.text: He never references this psalm anywhere in His teaching.
options.note: Jesus explicitly quotes this psalm's promise in the Beatitudes.

options.text: He reverses the promise, saying the proud will inherit the earth.
options.note: Jesus affirms rather than reverses the psalm's promise to the meek.

options.text: He applies the promise only to Israel's physical land, not any wider inheritance.
options.note: Jesus widens the promise from the land to the whole earth, not narrowing it.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's counsel for facing the wicked's apparent prosperity?

options.text: Do not fret or envy them, but trust, delight in, and wait patiently for the LORD.
options.note: This is the psalm's repeated refrain, given in verses 1, 3-5, 7-8, and 34.

options.text: Imitate their methods to gain similar success.
options.note: The psalm explicitly warns against envying or imitating the wicked, not adopting their ways.

options.text: Confront them publicly and demand justice immediately.
options.note: The psalm counsels stillness and patient trust in the LORD's timing, not immediate public confrontation.

options.text: Accept that God is indifferent to the outcome.
options.note: The psalm insists God sees, laughs at the wicked's schemes, and will certainly act, not that He is indifferent.

questions: The psalm opens by naming envy and fretting over the wicked as a real temptation. Where in your life are you tempted to envy someone whose prosperity seems to come from wrongdoing, and what would it look like to trust instead?

David says, 'Delight yourself in the LORD, and He will give you the desires of your heart.' How does delighting in God, rather than merely using Him to get what you want, actually reshape what your heart desires?

The psalm commends qavah, taut, expectant waiting, twice. What are you currently waiting for God to do, and how might expectant hope look different from either giving up or taking matters into your own hands?

David's testimony in verse 25 rests on a long life of observation. What have you personally observed, over years, about God's faithfulness to those who trust Him, even if the timing was not what you expected?

Jesus takes up this psalm's promise to the meek directly in the Beatitudes. How does knowing that Jesus Himself embodied perfect meekness and trust change the way you read David's counsel to commit your way to the LORD?

translationNotes.note: Fret renders a Hebrew root, charah, meaning to become heated or to kindle anger, picturing an inward burning agitation rather than mere worry.
Hope renders qavah, a word rooted in the image of a taut, stretched cord, describing expectant, straining hope rather than passive resignation.
Meek renders anavim, describing humility and dependence on God rather than mere gentleness of temperament; this is the exact word and promise Jesus repeats in Matthew 5:5.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 37:1-40. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch confirming the desiringgod.org listing for Psalm 37 messages.
